sentence: Subjective:

The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing, which he describes as a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, particularly with solids, and occasional pain when swallowing.
He reports that he has not experienced any coughing, vomiting, or blood in his stool.
He has a past medical history of diabetes and high blood pressure, for which he takes metformin 500mg twice a day and norvasc.
He reports that he has been following his medication regimen and diet, and his blood sugars have been within target range.
He also reports occasional lower blood pressure readings at home.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 120/50 mmHg, pulse rate not mentioned, oxygen saturation not mentioned, temperature not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine, with no swelling, thyroid gland is normal, no masses or lymph nodes are palpable.
The throat examination reveals no masses or swelling, no redness, and no carotid bruit.
The lung exam is clear, heart exam is normal, and no murmurs are present.
The belly exam shows epigastric tenderness on the right side, but no masses or significant swelling.
The back exam is normal, and the extremity exam is fine.
The neurological exam is normal.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has difficulty swallowing, suspected to be caused by a narrowing of the throat, possibly due to a stricture or narrowing of the esophagus.
Problem list: Difficulty swallowing; narrowing of the esophagus; diabetes; high blood pressure.
Plan:
I recommended that the patient see a gastroenterologist for further evaluation and possible dilation of the esophagus.
I also started him on prilosec 20mg once a day for reflux.
I advised him to chew his food frequently and slowly, especially with solids.
I ordered a hemoglobin A1c test and kidney function tests, and referred him to ophthalmology for a diabetic retinopathy evaluation.
I also provided a refill for norvasc.
Follow-up:
The patient will return in one month for a follow-up appointment to review the results of the blood tests and make any necessary adjustments to his medication regimen.
